chunk_summary: Human milk is the optimal nutrition for term and preterm infants, with a feeding hierarchy prioritizing mother’s milk, donor milk, and formula. Active maternal lactation support and daily reassessment of feeding plans are essential, especially in NICU settings.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical rationale for prioritizing human milk in neonatal nutrition.
Learn the feeding hierarchy for high-risk infants, including the role of donor milk and formula.
Identify strategies to support maternal lactation in NICU settings.
Recognize the importance of daily reassessment of feeding plans based on clinical status.
key_facts: Mother’s own milk is associated with lower rates of late-onset sepsis and necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC) in preterm infants.
Pasteurized donor human milk (DHM) is preferred for infants <32 weeks’ gestation or <1500 g when mother’s milk is unavailable.
The NICU feeding hierarchy is: mother’s milk (fresh > refrigerated > frozen), followed by pasteurized DHM, and preterm formula as a last resort.
Frequent expression (8–10 sessions/day) and skin-to-skin care are critical for establishing maternal milk supply.
Donor milk use should be time-limited and reassessed as maternal supply improves and clinical risk decreases.
reference_quote: Pasteurized donor human milk (DHM) is the preferred bridge, especially for infants <32 weeks’ gestation or <1500 g, because the protective effect against NEC is stronger than with formula.
document_key_topics: Feeding hierarchy for human milk in NICU
Clinical benefits of mother’s milk and donor milk
